title: The Lens Space Realization Problem
overall_summary: Greene proves the lens space realization theorem: every lens space obtained by positive integer surgery from a knot in S3 is realized, at the level of the relevant homology class and knot Floer data, by a Berge knot. The proof combines Heegaard Floer correction terms, Donaldson diagonalization, and a detailed combinatorial classification of changemaker lattices whose orthogonal complements are linear plumbing lattices. For the original problem, the most useful material is the lattice-theoretic core in Sections 3 and 4: irreducible elements in graph lattices are characterized by connected subsets with connected complements; vertex-basis elements in linear lattices are irreducible; changemaker standard-basis elements are proved irreducible by coordinate sign arguments; and intersection graphs of irreducible interval representatives satisfy strong forbidden-configuration constraints. Although the paper does not treat arbitrary weighted trees with one bad vertex directly, it supplies several portable methods for proving irreducibility of distinguished generators in positive-definite graph/plumbing lattices.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.1 observation: minimal-norm irreducible basis and pairing-graph decomposition
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a positive-definite integral lattice, meaning a finitely generated free abelian group equipped with a positive-definite symmetric bilinear pairing ⟨ , ⟩ taking integer values. For v in L write |v| = ⟨v,v⟩. A nonzero vector v is reducible if v = x + y for nonzero x,y in L with ⟨x,y⟩ ≥ 0, and irreducible otherwise. Then L has a basis S = {v1,...,vn} consisting entirely of irreducible vectors, obtained by choosing v1 of minimal positive norm and then choosing vi of minimal norm among vectors not in the span of v1,...,v_{i-1}. If S is an irreducible basis and its pairing graph has vertices S and edges between pairs with nonzero pairing, then the spans of the connected components of this graph are indecomposable orthogonal summands of L. The decomposition into indecomposable summands is unique up to reordering, by Eichler’s uniqueness theorem as cited in the paper.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the irreducible-basis construction, suppose vi is chosen of minimal norm outside the span W of the previous vectors and vi = x + y with x,y nonzero and ⟨x,y⟩ ≥ 0. Positive definiteness gives |vi| = |x| + |y| + 2⟨x,y⟩, so |x| < |vi| and |y| < |vi|. By minimality of vi, both x and y must lie in W, forcing vi in W, a contradiction. Thus every selected vi is irreducible. For the pairing-graph decomposition, different graph components are orthogonal by definition, so their spans form an orthogonal direct sum. If the span Lk of one connected component decomposed nontrivially as L′ ⊕ L″, then every irreducible basis vector in that component must lie wholly in one summand, since a vector with nonzero components in both summands would split orthogonally and hence be reducible. Connectedness of the component then forces all its basis vectors into the same summand, contradicting nontriviality. The cited Eichler theorem gives uniqueness of the resulting indecomposable decomposition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.2 construction: graph lattices and norm formula
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G = (V,E) be a finite loopless undirected graph, allowing parallel edges, and write e(v,w) for the number of edges between distinct vertices v,w. Define Γ(G) to be the free abelian group generated by symbols [v], v in V, with symmetric bilinear form given by ⟨[v],[v]⟩ = d(v), the degree of v, and ⟨[v],[w]⟩ = -e(v,w) for v ≠ w. For x = Σ_v x_v [v], one has |x| = Σ_{edges e=(v,w)} (x_v - x_w)^2. Hence Γ(G) is positive semidefinite, and its nullspace is generated by the sums [V_i] over connected components V_i of G. The graph lattice associated to G is the quotient Γ(G) = Γ(G)/Z(G), where Z(G) is this nullspace; it is positive definite. If G is connected and r is a chosen root vertex, then Γ(G) is identified with the span of {[v] : v ≠ r}, giving a vertex basis.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Expanding the defining form gives Σ_v d(v)x_v^2 - 2Σ_{edges (v,w)} x_v x_w, which is exactly Σ_{edges (v,w)}(x_v - x_w)^2. This expression is nonnegative and vanishes precisely when x_v is constant on each connected component. Thus the radical consists of the component-sums [V_i]. After quotienting by this radical the form is positive definite. In the connected case, the relation [V] = 0 in the quotient lets every class be written uniquely using all vertex generators except a fixed root r.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a connected finite loopless undirected graph with chosen root r, and let Γ(G) be its graph lattice with vertex basis {[v] : v in V minus {r}}. For a subset T of V, write [T] for the class Σ_{v in T}[v] in Γ(G), understanding that [T] = -[V minus T] if r lies in T. Then the irreducible elements of Γ(G) are exactly the vectors ±[T] for which both induced subgraphs G|T and G|(V minus T) are connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First show every irreducible vector has the form ±[T]. Write a nonzero x in the rooted representative as x = Σ_{v ≠ r} c_v [v]. Replacing x by -x if necessary, assume c = max c_v ≥ 1, and let T = {v : c_v = c}. Then T does not contain the root. A direct calculation gives ⟨[T], x - [T]⟩ = Σ_{v outside T} (c - 1 - c_v)e(v,T), which is nonnegative because c_v ≤ c-1 outside T. Since x is irreducible, the decomposition x = [T] + (x - [T]) forces x = [T]. Now determine when [T] is irreducible. For y = Σ y_v[v], with the rooted convention y_r = 0, the paper uses the identity ⟨y, y - [T]⟩ = Σ_C Σ_{(u,v) in E(C)}(y_u - y_v)^2 + Σ_{(u,v) in E(T,V minus T)}(y_u - y_v)(y_u - y_v - 1), where C ranges over connected components of G|T and G|(V minus T). Each summand is nonnegative for integer y_u - y_v. If G|(V minus T) is disconnected, choose a component C not containing r and put y = -[C]; then y and [T] - y are nonzero, orthogonal, and sum to [T], so [T] is reducible. If G|T is disconnected, choose a component C of G|T and put y = [C], again splitting [T] orthogonally. Conversely, if both induced subgraphs are connected and [T] = y + z with ⟨y,z⟩ ≥ 0, then ⟨y,y-[T]⟩ = -⟨y,z⟩ ≤ 0, while the displayed formula is nonnegative, so all summands vanish. Connectivity forces y to be constant on T and on V minus T, with value 0 on the root side and value either 0 or 1 on T; hence y is 0 or [T]. Thus no nontrivial reducible decomposition exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a connected graph with no cut-edge, with graph lattice Γ(G) rooted at r. Suppose a class [T] in Γ(G) satisfies [T] = y + z for y,z in Γ(G) with ⟨y,z⟩ = -1. Then exactly one of the following structural alternatives occurs. First, G|T contains a cut-edge e such that, writing the vertex sets of the two components of G|T minus e as T1 and T2, the unordered pair {y,z} equals {[T1],[T2]}. Second, G|(V minus T) contains a cut-edge e such that, writing the vertex sets of the two components of G|(V minus T) minus e as T1 and T2 with r in T2, the unordered pair {y,z} equals {[T1 union T], -[T1]}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the same identity as in Proposition 3.3. Since [T] = y + z and ⟨y,z⟩ = -1, one has ⟨y,y-[T]⟩ = 1. The summands (y_u-y_v)(y_u-y_v-1) across the cut between T and its complement are always 0 or at least 2 for integer differences, while the internal edge summands are squares. Therefore exactly one internal edge, either inside G|T or inside G|(V minus T), contributes 1, and every other term vanishes. This unique edge must be a cut-edge of the relevant induced subgraph. If the edge lies in G|T, then y is constant on each of the two components T1,T2 and on V minus T. Since G itself has no cut-edge, each Ti has some edge to V minus T, forcing the constants on T1 and T2 to differ from the outside constant by at most one and to differ from each other by exactly one; after using the root normalization, the only possibility is {y,z} = {[T1],[T2]}. If the edge lies in G|(V minus T), the same constant-value analysis, with the component T2 containing the root normalized to value 0, gives {y,z} = {[T1 union T], -[T1]}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L = direct sum over k of L_k be a sum of linear lattices, where each L_k has a vertex basis x1,...,xn with pairings ⟨xi,xi⟩ = a_i ≥ 2, ⟨xi,x_{i+1}⟩ = -1, and all other off-diagonal pairings zero. In such a linear summand, an interval means a consecutive set {xi,...,xj}, and [T] is the sum of the basis vectors in the interval. Then: (1) the irreducible vectors in L are exactly ±[T], where T is an interval in one summand; in particular every vertex-basis element xi is irreducible; (2) each linear summand L_k is indecomposable; (3) if two intervals T and T′ overlap but do not share a common endpoint, then [T minus T′] ± [T′ minus T] is reducible; (4) [T] is unbreakable, meaning it is not expressible as x+y with |x|,|y| ≥ 3 and ⟨x,y⟩ = -1, if and only if T contains at most one vertex of norm at least 3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Represent each linear lattice as a graph lattice by taking a path of vertices x1,...,xn, adding a root r, and adding enough edges from r to xi so that the graph degree of xi is a_i. Proposition 3.3 then says irreducibles correspond to connected subsets with connected complement. In this graph model those subsets are exactly intervals in a single path summand, proving (1). The pairing graph of the interval singleton basis in each summand is connected and the basis vectors are irreducible, so the indecomposability argument from Section 3.1 proves (2). For (3), if T and T′ overlap without common endpoint, their symmetric difference is a union of two distant intervals T1 and T2. Thus [T minus T′] ± [T′ minus T] is a sum of two nonzero orthogonal interval classes up to signs, hence reducible. For (4), if T contains two vertices of norm at least 3, split T into consecutive subintervals T1,T2 each containing one such vertex; then [T]=[T1]+[T2], both norms are at least 3, and ⟨[T1],[T2]⟩=-1, so [T] is breakable. Conversely, if [T] is breakable, Proposition 3.4 applies in the graph model. The case where the cut-edge lies in the complement would yield one summand of norm 2, incompatible with breakability. Thus the cut-edge lies inside T, splitting T into two intervals of norms at least 3, and each such interval must contain a vertex of norm at least 3. Hence T contains at least two high-norm vertices.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T = {T1,...,Tk} be a collection of intervals in a linear lattice such that the interval classes [Ti] are linearly independent. Define a graph G(T) whose vertices are the intervals Ti and whose edges join pairs of intervals that abut, meaning that they are consecutive or share a common endpoint. If C is a cycle in G(T), then all intervals in V(C) abut pairwise at a common end: there is an index j such that every Ti in V(C) has left endpoint x_{j+1} or right endpoint x_j. In particular, V(C) induces a complete subgraph of G(T).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on the number of edges in the subgraph induced by the intervals of the cycle. If the cycle is induced and no three intervals abut at a common end, orient the cyclic list T1,...,Tk and assign signs ε_i = ±1 according to whether Ti lies to the right of Ti−1 or shares the appropriate common endpoint. For each basis vertex x_j, the contributions of intervals containing x_j cancel in pairs because the next interval around the cycle containing x_j has the opposite sign. Hence Σ_i ε_i[Ti] = 0, contradicting linear independence. Thus the base case is a triangle with all three intervals abutting at a common end. If the induced subgraph has a chord, split the cycle into two smaller cycles along the chord and apply induction to each; both force the same common end for the chord intervals, hence for all intervals in the original cycle.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Subsection 3.4 construction: changemaker lattice standard basis
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let σ = (σ0,...,σn) in Z^{n+1} be a changemaker, meaning 1 = σ0 ≤ σ1 ≤ ... ≤ σn and every integer between 0 and σ0+...+σn is a subset sum of the σi; equivalently σi ≤ σ0+...+σ_{i-1}+1 for every i ≥ 1. In the Euclidean lattice Z^{n+1} with orthonormal basis e0,...,en, let L = (σ)⊥ be the orthogonal complement of σ. For each 1 ≤ j ≤ n, define v_j as follows. If σ_j = 1 + Σ_{i=0}^{j-1} σ_i, set v_j = -e_j + 2e0 + Σ_{i=1}^{j-1} e_i and call it tight. Otherwise choose, among all subsets A of {0,...,j-1} with σ_j = Σ_{i in A} σ_i, the subset maximal in binary order, and set v_j = -e_j + Σ_{i in A} e_i. In the latter case v_j is called gappy if A is not a set of consecutive integers and just right if A is consecutive. Then S = {v1,...,vn} is a basis of L, called the standard basis.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Each v_j is orthogonal to σ by construction. The vectors are triangular with coefficient -1 on e_j and no e_i with i > j, so they are linearly independent. To see that they span L, take any w in L and successively eliminate its e_n, e_{n-1},...,e_1 coordinates by adding suitable integer multiples of v_n, v_{n-1},...,v_1. The resulting vector lies in L and is supported only on e0, hence is zero because σ0 = 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the standard basis S = {v1,...,vn} of a changemaker lattice L = (σ)⊥ as above, write v_{j,k} = ⟨v_j,e_k⟩. The following hold. (1) v_{j,j} = -1 for all j, and v_{j,j-1} = 1 unless j = 1 and v_{1,0} = 2. (2) For any pair vi,vj in S, one has ⟨vi,vj⟩ ≥ -1. (3) If k is a gappy index for a gappy vector v_j, meaning k lies in the chosen subset A but k+1 does not lie in A union {j}, then |v_{k+1}| ≥ 3. (4) If z in L has |z| ≥ 3, has exactly one negative coordinate, namely z_j = -1, then j is the maximum index in supp(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Part (1) is immediate from the triangular construction and the maximal choice of A: except in the tight one-dimensional case, the largest possible previous index j-1 must appear. Part (2) follows because each v_j has coefficient -1 in its own new coordinate and nonnegative coefficients in earlier coordinates, so two different standard-basis vectors can have at most one negative contribution to their inner product. For (3), if v_{k+1} had norm 2, then v_{k+1} would allow replacing the occurrence of e_k in v_j by a lexicographically larger subset representation of σ_j, contradicting maximality of A. For (4), if some k > j lay in supp(z) with positive coefficient, then 0 = ⟨z,σ⟩ would be strictly greater than σ_k - σ_j, which is nonnegative since σ is nondecreasing; this contradiction proves that no support occurs beyond j.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every standard-basis element v_j of a changemaker lattice L = (σ)⊥ is irreducible in L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume v_j = x + y with x,y in L nonzero and ⟨x,y⟩ ≥ 0, and write coordinates in the orthonormal e_i basis. If v_j is not tight, then every coordinate of v_j is 0, 1, or -1. If any coordinate product x_i y_i were positive, then the corresponding coordinate of v_j would have absolute value at least 2, impossible. Since ⟨x,y⟩ is the sum of these products and is nonnegative, all coordinate products must be zero. Because v_j has only one negative coordinate, one of x and y has all coordinates nonnegative. A nonzero vector in (σ)⊥ cannot have all coordinates nonnegative because all σ_i are positive, so one summand is zero, contradiction. If v_j is tight, the only coordinate where both summands can have the same positive sign is e0, forcing x0 = y0 = 1. There can be at most one coordinate k with x_k y_k = -1; otherwise the inner product would be negative. If no such coordinate exists, the previous nonnegative-coordinate argument applies. If such k exists, assume x_k = 1 and y_k = -1. Then y has its unique negative coordinate at k, while x has its unique negative coordinate at j. Since tight v_j has nonzero coordinates for all indices up to j, this cancellation coordinate k must satisfy k > j. But x has norm at least 3 and unique negative coordinate at j, so Lemma 3.12(4) says j is the maximum index in supp(x), contradicting k > j. Thus v_j is irreducible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 3.14 and 3.15
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L = (σ)⊥ be a changemaker lattice with standard basis S. Suppose v_t is tight. Then the paper proves three additional irreducibility tests: (1) if v_j is also tight and j ≠ t, then v_j - v_t is irreducible; (2) if j > t and v_j = -e_j + e_{j-1} + e_t, then v_j + v_t is irreducible; (3) if v_{t+1} = -e_{t+1} + e_t + ... + e0, then v_{t+1} - v_t is irreducible. Separately, if v_j is not tight, then v_j is unbreakable: there do not exist x,y in L with v_j = x+y, |x|,|y| ≥ 3, and ⟨x,y⟩ = -1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proofs are coordinate-sign refinements of Lemma 3.13. For v_j - v_t with two tight vectors, one may assume j > t; the vector has a coefficient 2 at e_t, positive coefficients between t+1 and j-1, and coefficient -1 at e_j. A reducible decomposition with nonnegative pairing would force both summands to have e_t coefficient 1 and a single cancellation coordinate k. Lemma 3.12(4), applied to the summand with unique negative coordinate, forces incompatible inequalities among k,t,j. The cases v_j+v_t and v_{t+1}-v_t are similar: the possible coordinate products are so constrained that one summand would have a unique negative coordinate whose index is not maximal, or would pair with σ with the wrong sign. For unbreakability of a non-tight v_j, a breakable decomposition v_j=x+y with ⟨x,y⟩=-1 and |x|,|y|≥3 has exactly one coordinate where x_i y_i=-1 and all other coordinate products zero. Lemma 3.12(4) then forces the negative-coordinate indices to be maximal in contradictory ways, and the maximality built into the standard-basis choice gives v_j strictly smaller than one summand in the binary order, impossible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2 and Corollary 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume a changemaker lattice L = (σ)⊥ with standard basis S is isomorphic to a linear lattice or a direct sum of linear lattices. By Lemma 3.13 and Corollary 3.5, each v_i in S can be written as ε_i[T_i], where ε_i is ±1 and T_i is an interval in a linear summand. If v_t is tight, then: (1) no other standard-basis vector is tight; (2) if j > t+1 and v_j = -e_j + e_{j-1} + e_t, then the intervals T_t and T_j are consecutive; (3) if v_{t+1} = -e_{t+1} + e_t + ... + e0, then t = 1 and T_1 and T_2 are consecutive. Consequently, using Lemma 3.15, a standard basis contains at most one breakable vector, and if it exists it is tight.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1), if v_j and v_t were both tight, then Lemma 3.14 says v_j - v_t is irreducible. But the pairings of two tight vectors force their interval representatives to overlap transversely in the linear-lattice model, and Corollary 3.5(3) says the corresponding interval difference is reducible, a contradiction. For (2), the coordinate pairing gives ⟨v_t,v_j⟩=-1 and |v_j|=3. The interval alternatives are either that T_t and T_j are consecutive, or that the unique high-norm vertex of T_j lies inside T_t with transverse overlap. The latter would make ε_j v_j - ε_t v_t equal a reducible symmetric-difference vector, while Lemma 3.14 says v_j+v_t is irreducible; hence only consecutiveness remains. For (3), the same argument applies to v_{t+1}-v_t, using Lemma 3.14 and excluding the transverse-overlap alternative. Finally, Lemma 3.15 says every non-tight standard-basis vector is unbreakable, and (1) says there is at most one tight vector, so at most one standard-basis vector can be breakable.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4 and Corollary 4.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the same setting where a changemaker lattice with standard basis S is isomorphic to a sum of linear lattices, let v_i = ε_i[T_i] and v_j = ε_j[T_j] be unbreakable standard-basis elements with |v_i|,|v_j| ≥ 3. Then |⟨v_i,v_j⟩| ≤ 1. Equality holds if and only if the intervals T_i and T_j are consecutive, and in that case ε_i ε_j = -⟨v_i,v_j⟩. As a consequence, if T_i and T_j are distinct unbreakable intervals of norm at least 3, then their unique high-norm vertices z_i and z_j are distinct.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The main point is to prove ⟨[T_i],[T_j]⟩ ≤ 0. If this interval pairing were positive, unbreakability implies that T_i and T_j share the same unique vertex of degree at least 3. If one vector is tight, direct coordinate bounds show that the changemaker pairing has absolute value at most d-3 while the interval pairing has size at least d-2, impossible. If neither vector is tight and the signs ε_i,ε_j agree, then positive pairing forces either one interval nearly to contain the other or a transverse overlap with almost identical supports; in the first case one obtains a lexicographically larger allowed standard-basis vector such as v_j - v_i, and in the second case v_j - v_i is irreducible by the same coordinate argument as Lemma 3.13 but reducible by Corollary 3.5(3). If the signs are opposite, the only remaining positive interval-pairing case has norm 3 and transverse overlap; then v_j+v_i is coordinate-irreducible but corresponds to a reducible symmetric-difference vector. These contradictions force nonpositive interval pairing. The statement about equality follows because for unbreakable intervals of norms at least 3, the only way to get pairing -1 is for the intervals to be consecutive, with the sign relation determined by v_i = ε_i[T_i]. The corollary follows immediately: if two such intervals shared the same high-norm vertex, their interval pairing would be positive.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.8, 4.10, and 4.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of a changemaker lattice L = (σ)⊥ isomorphic to a sum of linear lattices, remove the possible single breakable standard-basis vector and call the remaining set S_bar. Write each v_i in S_bar as ε_i[T_i], with T_i an interval in a linear summand. Define the intersection graph G(S_bar) with vertices S_bar and edges between v_i,v_j when the intervals T_i,T_j abut. Then: (1) G(S_bar) is claw-free, meaning it has no induced K_{1,3}; (2) G(S_bar) contains no heavy triple, where a heavy triple is a set of three distinct norm-at-least-3 vertices in the same connected component such that none separates the other two in the graph; (3) every cycle in G(S_bar) has length 3 and contains a unique vector of norm 2. Moreover, if (v_i,v_j,v_k) is such a cycle with i < j < k and v_k is not gappy, then all v_l with l ≤ i have norm 2, except that if a breakable vector v_t was removed the conclusion is for t < l ≤ i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Claw-freeness is purely interval-theoretic: if one interval abuts three others, at least two of those abut it at the same end, and therefore they abut each other, so the claw is not induced. For the heavy-triple exclusion, place the relevant intervals in the same path of the linear lattice. Each unbreakable interval of norm at least 3 contains a unique high-norm vertex. If z_i lies between z_j and z_k along the path, then any unbreakable interval avoiding z_i lies entirely to one side of it; hence v_i separates v_j from v_k in the intersection graph, contradicting the definition of heavy triple. For cycles, Lemma 3.8 first implies that the intervals in any cycle induce a complete subgraph, all abutting pairwise at a common end. Such a complete subgraph cannot contain three norm-at-least-3 vertices because that would be a heavy triangle. Norm-2 standard-basis vectors themselves induce only paths, and two norm-2 vertices in the same cycle would have forms v_{i+1}=-e_{i+1}+e_i and v_i=-e_i+e_{i-1}; any third interval abutting both would contradict maximality of the standard-basis representation by allowing replacement by v_j-v_i or v_j-v_{i+1}. Thus a cycle has exactly two high-norm vertices and one norm-2 vertex, so it is a triangle. The final initial-segment norm-2 assertion follows by inspecting the support of the largest-index non-gappy vector in such a triangle and observing that any earlier high-norm vector would create a forbidden heavy triple.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.15
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L = (σ)⊥ be a changemaker lattice with standard basis S, isomorphic to a linear lattice or a sum of linear lattices. Suppose v_s is chosen with s smallest such that |v_s| ≥ 3, and suppose v_s is not tight. Then v_s is just right, and |v_s| is either s or s+1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If v_s were gappy, then by Lemma 3.12(3) a gappy index k for v_s would force |v_{k+1}| ≥ 3 with k+1 < s, contradicting the minimal choice of s. Thus v_s is just right. A just-right vector has the form -e_s + e_{s-1} + ... + e_k. If |v_s| < s, then k lies between 2 and s-2. The vectors v_{k-1}, v_{k+1}, and v_s all abut v_k in the intersection graph while not all abutting one another, giving an induced claw. This contradicts Lemma 4.8. Therefore the consecutive support has length s or s+1, i.e. |v_s| is s or s+1.

proof_techniques.name: Minimal-norm irreducibility induction
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the elementary fact that in a positive-definite lattice a vector chosen with minimal norm outside a fixed sublattice cannot split as x+y with ⟨x,y⟩ ≥ 0. Such a splitting would make both x and y have strictly smaller norm than the chosen vector, forcing them into the previous span and contradicting linear independence. This gives an irreducible basis in every positive-definite lattice and supplies a way to prove irreducibility by contradiction using norm minimality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a positive-definite weighted tree lattice, one can try choosing vertices or combinations with minimal norm or minimal support and showing that any reducible splitting would produce smaller-norm lattice elements confined to a previously controlled sublattice. This is especially relevant if an induction on deleting leaves or arms is set up.

proof_techniques.name: Graph-laplacian energy formula
proof_techniques.description: For graph lattices with diagonal entries equal to vertex degrees and off-diagonal entries minus edge multiplicities, Greene uses the identity |Σ x_v[v]| = Σ_{edges (v,w)}(x_v-x_w)^2. This turns lattice inequalities into combinatorial statements about integer functions on vertices. The formula also identifies the radical as functions constant on connected components and makes connectivity conditions visible in the quadratic form.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original tree form differs from the graph-laplacian form by diagonal corrections w(v)-d(v). Since the hypothesis includes a vertex with w(v)<d(v), comparing the given form to the graph-laplacian energy may expose vectors of small or nonpositive norm under an assumption that all vertices are reducible.

proof_techniques.name: Connected-subset criterion for irreducibility
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 3.3 proves irreducibility in graph lattices by showing that any irreducible must be ±[T], and that [T] is irreducible exactly when both T and its complement induce connected subgraphs. The key calculation is the nonnegative sum formula for ⟨y,y-[T]⟩, whose vanishing forces y to be constant on connected components. Disconnected induced subgraphs give explicit orthogonal decompositions, while connectedness prevents them.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A weighted tree proof may imitate this by analyzing a putative vertex decomposition u=a+b through the connected components of the support of a or b after deleting u. The bad-vertex condition might be used to rule out all possible component-wise splittings unless some vertex is irreducible.

proof_techniques.name: Linear-lattice interval model
proof_techniques.description: Linear plumbing lattices are modeled as graph lattices of a path plus a root. This converts irreducible elements into interval sums and makes vertex-basis irreducibility immediate. Breakability is also interval-theoretic: an interval class is breakable exactly when the interval contains at least two vertices of norm at least 3.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Any tree can be decomposed into path arms from a chosen vertex. The interval model suggests proving irreducibility first along arms or using interval sums as canonical pieces in any reducible decomposition of a vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Coordinate-sign obstruction in Euclidean embeddings
proof_techniques.description: For changemaker lattices L=(σ)⊥ inside a standard Euclidean lattice, standard-basis vectors have one negative coordinate and controlled nonnegative coordinates. Greene proves these vectors irreducible by observing that a reducible splitting with nonnegative pairing severely restricts coordinate products; one summand must have all coordinates nonnegative, or a unique negative coordinate whose index violates a support-maximality lemma. This is a powerful local sign-pattern argument independent of the global classification.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the weighted tree lattice can be embedded into a diagonal lattice, or if local vertex vectors can be represented with one negative coordinate and positive tails, this sign argument can be ported to show specific vertices cannot split reducibly.

proof_techniques.name: Comparing two irreducible bases via intervals
proof_techniques.description: When a changemaker lattice is also a sum of linear lattices, every changemaker standard-basis vector is irreducible and hence must be an interval sum in the linear-lattice vertex basis. Greene studies the resulting collection of intervals, using signs, overlaps, and endpoint relations to derive strong restrictions. This comparison is the engine behind the classification of standard bases.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A possible strategy for the original problem is to assume no vertex is irreducible, choose another irreducible basis by minimal norm, and compare the original vertex generating set to irreducible pieces. Forbidden interval or support configurations may then contradict the presence of a bad vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Intersection graph forbidden configurations
proof_techniques.description: The paper encodes interval representatives of irreducible standard-basis vectors in an intersection graph whose edges mean the intervals abut. The graph is claw-free, has no heavy triples, and has only triangular cycles with a unique norm-2 vertex. These restrictions are derived from interval combinatorics plus irreducibility and unbreakability.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Weighted trees with a high-valence or bad vertex naturally create claw-like local configurations. If reducibility of all vertices forces interval representatives around that vertex, the paper’s claw-free and heavy-triple principles suggest a route to contradiction.

proof_techniques.name: Expansion and reduction of standard bases
proof_techniques.description: Greene’s later sections classify standard bases by repeatedly applying expansions, operations that add a new vector of the form -e_m+e_{m-1}+...+e_k under norm-2 chain conditions. Expansions preserve the linear/changemaker structure and reduce complicated bases to small initial configurations. Although the detailed classification is lens-space-specific, the method is a disciplined induction on adding or deleting leaves in a plumbing-like lattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a weighted tree, an induction that deletes leaves or contracts norm-2 chains may mirror the expansion-reduction method. One could try to reduce to a small bad-vertex configuration where irreducibility of a vertex is direct.

other_useful_info: Terminology from the paper that may be useful: a vector is breakable if it splits as x+y with |x|,|y| ≥ 3 and ⟨x,y⟩ = -1; otherwise it is unbreakable. In linear lattices, breakability of an interval is exactly the presence of two high-norm vertices in that interval. The paper cites Milnor-Husemoller for uniqueness of indecomposable lattice decompositions, Gerstein for isomorphism classification of linear lattices, and Lisca’s work for expansion-style combinatorial analysis of lattice embeddings.
why_relevant: Introduces changemaker lattices and the combinatorial analysis of standard bases in positive-definite lattices. These methods are central to later proofs detecting irreducible elements in plumbing and graph lattices.
